lesson_title: Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.text: When you create a derived class object, it contains {{c1::multiple parts}}: one part for {{c2::each inherited class}}, and a part for {{c3::itself}}.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.front: Can you set a Base pointer or reference to a Derived object in C++?
cards.back: Yes, this is legal. Since Derived has a Base part (is-a relationship), you can point a Base* or Base& to a Derived object.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Even though pBase points to a Derived object, it can only see Base members. It calls Base::getName(), not Derived::getName().
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.text: A Base pointer or reference can only see {{c1::members of Base}} (or any classes that Base inherited), not members of the derived class.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.front: What is one advantage of using base class pointers/references with derived objects?
cards.back:
You can write a single function that works with all derived classes, instead of writing overloaded functions for each derived type.

Example: One report(const Animal&) function works for Cat, Dog, and any future Animal-derived classes.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.question: What problem exists with this code?
cards.answer: It calls Animal::speak() instead of Cat::speak() or Dog::speak(), because animal is a pointer to Animal.
cards.explanation: The base class pointer can only see Animal members, so it calls the base version of speak(), not the derived versions that would return "Meow" or "Woof".
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.text: When a Base pointer points to a Derived object and calls a function, it calls the {{c1::Base}} version of the function, not the {{c2::Derived}} version.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.front: What advantage does storing derived objects as base class pointers in an array provide?
cards.back:
You can store objects of different derived types in a single array, instead of needing separate arrays for each type.

Example: std::array<Animal*> can hold both Cat* and Dog* objects.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.concept: Why use base class references in function parameters
cards.explanation:
Using base class references allows a single function to accept any derived type, making code more flexible and maintainable.

Without base references, you need separate overloaded functions for each derived type, which becomes unmaintainable with many derived classes.
examples: void report(const Animal& a) { }  // Works for Cat, Dog, etc.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.text: Derived::getName() {{c1::shadows}} (or hides) Base::getName() for Derived objects, but a Base pointer cannot see the {{c2::derived}} version.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.front: What are two issues with using a template function instead of base class references for polymorphism?
cards.back:
• It's not clear what type T should be (lost documentation that T is an Animal)
  • The function doesn't enforce that T is an Animal - it accepts any type with the required member functions
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

cards.concept: The fundamental problem with base class pointers/references
cards.explanation:
Base class pointers and references can only call the base class version of functions, not the derived class versions, even when pointing to a derived object.

This prevents polymorphic behavior and is exactly the problem that virtual functions solve.
cards.source: 25.1 - Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects
